name: Amy Beliveau
background: Real estate and development professional with more than a decade of experience in development, design and negotiation. Beliveau said she has served on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee; a forum article identified her as a six-year resident and Town Meeting member from precinct seven.
summary: Beliveau ran for Planning Board in 2026 emphasizing balanced growth, real estate and development expertise, and data-driven collaboration. In campaign materials and a candidate forum, she argued Winchester should add housing thoughtfully, including beyond transit-oriented corridors, while honoring town history. She won one of two open seats, finishing ahead of incumbent Nicholas Rossettos.
locationNote: Winchester resident; described in forum coverage as a six-year resident and Town Meeting member representing precinct seven.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and town committee service
priorRecordSummary: Beliveau pointed to service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee as giving her a broad understanding of town government, but the provided sources do not document a prior Planning Board voting record.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided materials are about the Planning Board race and do not show a stated position from Beliveau on school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Data-driven, financially informed planning
stanceCards.detail: Beliveau said she is a data-driven decision maker and emphasized understanding development finance and the policy levers the town can use to create value and meet objectives.

stanceCards.label: Supports added housing options
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, Beliveau said Winchester should add housing and urged looking beyond MBTA Communities transit-oriented areas, including conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Candidate profile highlights development background and collaborative approach
evidence.summary: Beliveau said she would bring more than a decade of development, design and negotiation experience, plus service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee. She described herself as data-driven and supportive of collaboration among developers, design experts, historians and town officials to guide growth.

evidence.title: Forum: Beliveau argues for housing growth beyond transit corridors
evidence.summary: In a League of Women Voters forum, Beliveau said the board needs real estate industry knowledge and argued that housing opportunity and historic value are not defined simply by building size. She urged the town to look beyond MBTA Communities transit-oriented corridors and consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Official election results show Beliveau won second Planning Board seat
evidence.summary: Official town results list Keri Layton with 2,331 votes, Amy S. Beliveau with 2,042, and Nicholas J. Rossettos with 1,864 for two open Planning Board seats, placing Beliveau in a winning position.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage says Beliveau unseated incumbent Rossettos
evidence.summary: Election-night reporting said Beliveau, described as a newcomer, unseated incumbent Nicholas Rossettos to claim one of the two open Planning Board seats.

name: Heather von Mering
background: Mother of five in Winchester Public Schools with experience across multiple grade levels, including out-of-district placement. Works as a vice president in construction and asset planning, and has served 16 years in Winchester town government, including on the Historical Commission, Planning Board, Capital Planning Committee, as Town Moderator, and as a Town Meeting Member.
summary: Von Mering ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing parent experience, literacy reform, special education advocacy, facilities planning, and municipal finance experience. She argued the district should focus on closing literacy and math gaps and called for careful, expert-informed work on technology policy. She lost the 2026 race to John Bellaire.
locationNote: Winchester resident; moved to Winchester in 2009.
priorRecordSummary: Her public record in the source packet is primarily through town-government service and Town Meeting budget action rather than prior School Committee service.

stanceCards.label: Backs literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: Described herself as a long-time advocate for literacy reform, supported the district’s new literacy curriculum, and said earlier identification and support for dyslexia can improve outcomes and reduce later costs.

stanceCards.label: Supported requested school budget
stanceCards.detail: At 2025 Town Meeting, she said Town Meeting should fund the school budget as requested and opposed using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget gap.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes finance and transparency
stanceCards.detail: Cited municipal finance, capital planning, transparency, and disciplined goal setting as strengths she would bring to the committee.

stanceCards.label: Facilities planning background
stanceCards.detail: Said her professional work in construction and asset planning informs her approach to capital planning, budgeting, and stewardship of school buildings.

stanceCards.label: Wants expert-informed technology policy
stanceCards.detail: Said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise on AI and technology policy rather than write policy without subject-matter expertise.

evidence.title: Candidate profile describes parent, advocacy, professional, and town-government experience
evidence.summary: Von Mering said she would bring perspectives as a parent of five in Winchester schools, a literacy and special education advocate, a construction and asset-planning professional, and a longtime town leader with municipal finance experience.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy and early intervention focus
evidence.summary: At the School Committee forum, she supported the literacy curriculum rollout, discussed dyslexia identification gaps, and argued early support lowers later costs.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on technology policy
evidence.summary: She said she was not an expert on AI and that the School Committee should rely on outside expertise when developing technology policy.

evidence.title: Town Meeting amendment on school budget
evidence.summary: In 2025, von Mering argued against using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget and moved to fund the School Committee’s requested budget with additional free cash to offset the deficit.

evidence.title: Election-night summary of candidate contrast
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said von Mering argued the district should close its literacy and math gaps before taking on new commitments, and that both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health and agreed the district lacked a coherent technology policy.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes and Heather R. von Mering received 1,861 in the School Committee race.

name: John Bellaire
background: Education researcher and Winchester Public Schools graduate who works for a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance. He has also worked as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary School and as Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance.
summary: Bellaire presented himself as a school-finance-focused education policy professional with local school experience and a recent Winchester student perspective. In campaign materials and a candidate forum, he emphasized equitable and transparent school finance, student opportunity, literacy improvement, classroom technology, and student mental health.
locationNote: Winchester Public Schools graduate; attended Muraco Elementary School, McCall Middle School, and Winchester High School.
priorRecordLabel: Education policy and local youth work
priorRecordSummary: Bellaire did not appear in the source packet with prior School Committee service, but he cited professional work on school finance, school quality reviews, substitute teaching in Winchester, and mentoring through Winchester A Better Chance.

stanceCards.label: Budget analysis and finance experience
stanceCards.detail: He said his professional background includes developing financial models to help build equitable and transparent school finance systems and that his experience interrogating school budgets would help him serve Winchester schools.

stanceCards.label: Push for faster literacy progress
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, he said Winchester has been "behind the curve and a bit delayed" on literacy and argued for earlier support for struggling students.

stanceCards.label: Support student mental health
stanceCards.detail: In his profile, he said his experience as a Winchester student motivates him to support student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Address classroom technology
stanceCards.detail: In his profile, he said his student experience motivates him to address technology in the classroom.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines education policy and youth mentoring background
evidence.summary: Bellaire described himself as an education researcher, youth mentor, Winchester Public Schools graduate, substitute teacher, and Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance. He said he works on equitable and transparent school finance systems and school quality reviews.

evidence.title: Profile cites student-centered priorities
evidence.summary: Bellaire said his experiences as a Winchester student motivate him to provide high-quality student engagement opportunities, address technology in the classroom, and support student mental health.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy and budget comments
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Bellaire said Winchester had been delayed on literacy, cited MCAS reading performance concerns, and argued that earlier intervention could better support students before they fall behind. He also cited budget data on special education spending and disability classification rates.

evidence.title: Forum answer frames broad student opportunity goal
evidence.summary: Bellaire said every student should have the opportunity to develop into their best selves and that he wants to support students in achieving the futures they envision for themselves.

evidence.title: Won 2026 School Committee election
evidence.summary: Official town election results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes to Heather R. von Mering's 1,861 in the race for one School Committee seat.

name: Keri Layton
background: Incumbent Planning Board member seeking a second term in 2026. Previously served as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member starting in 2017, on the Master Plan Steering Committee, and for three years on the Conservation Commission. She chaired the Planning Board during North Main Street rezoning work.
summary: Layton presented herself as an experienced planning and conservation-focused incumbent who helped advance major zoning changes, including MBTA Communities multifamily zoning, the Main Street Mixed Use District, and ADUs by right. In the campaign forum, she emphasized continued housing production, especially affordable housing, while spreading growth thoughtfully and setting clear expectations for developers.
locationNote: Winchester; previously represented Precinct 2 in Town Meeting and later appears in 2026 results as a Precinct 3 Town Meeting member.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent Planning Board chair and member
priorRecordSummary: Layton described serving on the Planning Board through MBTA Communities zoning implementation, Main Street Mixed Use District adoption, ADU by-right changes, and review of the 10 Converse Place / 33 Mount Vernon Street redevelopment. She was reelected in 2026, leading the field for two seats.
keyThemes: Balancing development with conservation and town character
Clearer coordination between Planning Board and Conservation Commission
Mixed-use development to expand commercial tax base

stanceCards.label: Links growth to broader town assets
stanceCards.detail: Layton said natural resources are as critical to Winchester as historic buildings and excellent schools, framing planning decisions as part of preserving town quality of life.

stanceCards.label: Supports more housing and affordable units
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, Layton said Winchester remained hundreds of subsidized units short of its state threshold and argued for steady production over time.

stanceCards.label: Sees mixed-use as fiscally helpful
stanceCards.detail: Layton said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

stanceCards.label: Wants clearer development expectations
stanceCards.detail: She said the board should use pre-application meetings to state affordable housing requirements, design expectations, and height limits early.

evidence.title: Layton outlines prior town service and planning rationale
evidence.summary: Layton said she served as a Town Meeting member, on the Master Plan Steering Committee, and on the Conservation Commission, and ran for Planning Board to improve how its work balances Conservation Commission interests.

evidence.title: Layton cites zoning accomplishments on Planning Board
evidence.summary: She said she chaired the board during implementation of MBTA Communities multifamily zoning, helped bring the Main Street Mixed Use District to Town Meeting, and supported ADUs by right.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on housing production and distribution
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Layton said Winchester was still about 400 subsidized units short and argued for creative ways to distribute housing across town rather than concentrating density only in a few corridors.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on mixed-use and project expectations
evidence.summary: Layton said mixed-use projects expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools, and said the town should set affordable housing, design, and height expectations early with developers.

evidence.title: 2026 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Layton won one of two Planning Board seats with 2,331 votes, the highest total in the race.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting confirms outcome
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported Layton led the Planning Board field and won reelection, while Amy Beliveau claimed the second open seat.

name: Michelle Prior
background: Incumbent Select Board member and chair at the time of the 2026 campaign. Prior said she had 15 years on Town Meeting representing Precinct 4, six years on the Finance Committee including chairing FY24, three years on the Select Board with two years as chair, and service on the Audit Advisory Committee and Retiree Health Insurance Advisory Committee. She also said she served five years on the Wright-Locke Farm Conservancy board.
summary: Prior ran for a second Select Board term emphasizing long-term financial planning, added non-property-tax revenue, support for varied housing options, and cross-board communication. She won reelection in 2026.
locationNote: Winchester town election profile and results.
priorRecordSummary: Official minutes show Prior serving as Select Board chair in May 2025. In her campaign profile, she described prior service on Town Meeting, the Finance Committee, and the Select Board. Election coverage described her as the incumbent chair and noted a prior six-year Finance Committee tenure.
keyThemes: housing options for different ages and incomes

stanceCards.label: Seeks non-property-tax revenue
stanceCards.detail: Prior called for generating revenue beyond the residential property tax levy through commercial growth, fee reviews, monetizing town assets, and lease opportunities such as solar roof space.

stanceCards.label: Supports housing options for varied residents
stanceCards.detail: She said the town should maintain a community where people of all ages and incomes can live and thrive, including housing that serves different family configurations and seniors aging in place.

stanceCards.label: Framed override ask as limited to identified needs
stanceCards.detail: During override negotiations, Prior argued the board should not ask voters for money beyond what modeling showed was necessary and resisted adding extra cushion 'just in case.'

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes long-term planning and process
stanceCards.detail: She said she was seeking another term to build a long-term financial plan, support the new town manager, improve communication, and continue cost-saving initiatives. Coverage also said she emphasized deliberate process and consensus-building.

stanceCards.label: Discussed cuts if override failed
stanceCards.detail: In her profile, Prior said final decisions would likely rest with the town manager, superintendent, and Town Meeting, and she would advise reviewing unfilled positions, retirement incentives, and modest staffing or hours reductions in departments that could operate with less staff.

evidence.title: Campaign profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Prior said she had served 15 years on Town Meeting, six years on the Finance Committee, and three years on the Select Board with two years as chair. She highlighted non-property-tax revenue, housing options, communication, and long-term financial planning.

evidence.title: Profile details fallback ideas if override failed
evidence.summary: Prior said she would advise reviewing unfilled positions, retirement-age incentives, and modest reductions in staffing or operating hours for departments able to function with a smaller workforce.

evidence.title: Official minutes show Prior as Select Board chair
evidence.summary: Joint School Committee and Select Board State of the Town minutes from May 30, 2025 list Michelle Prior as Select Board chair.

evidence.title: Official election results: Prior wins Select Board seat
evidence.summary: Official 2026 election results show Michelle I. Prior defeating Shamus M. Brady, 2,849 to 1,159, for the one Select Board seat.

evidence.title: Override negotiation coverage quotes Prior on limiting ask
evidence.summary: During February 2026 override negotiations, Prior argued against asking voters for more money than modeling showed was necessary and said pre-committing override funds to the health care stabilization fund would reduce flexibility.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage describes campaign framing
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said Prior, the incumbent chair, won in a landslide and had emphasized deliberate process and consensus-building throughout her campaign.

name: Nick Rossettos
background: Incumbent Planning Board member from Winchester who said he had spent the prior four years working on mixed-use development, affordable housing approvals, MBTA Communities zoning compliance, and design standards. He described himself as a fractional CFO for life sciences companies and emphasized a lifelong interest in Winchester’s historic design.
summary: Rossettos ran for reelection to the Planning Board in 2026 highlighting experience with mixed-use and affordable housing projects, zoning reform, and design review. His campaign message stressed that Winchester can add housing while preserving downtown character and using careful design standards. He finished third in a three-candidate race for two seats.
locationNote: Grew up in Winchester and graduated from Winchester High School.
priorRecordSummary: Rossettos said he had worked for four years on mixed-use development approvals, MBTA 3A zoning compliance, North Main Street zoning, and design standards. At the candidate forum he was identified as an incumbent and as liaison to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.

stanceCards.label: Backs affordable and inclusionary housing
stanceCards.detail: He pointed to the unanimously approved Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use development and said North Main Street zoning prioritized inclusionary housing and mixed-use development.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities platform in sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources are about Planning Board issues and do not describe a position on school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Sees development as a way to expand tax base
stanceCards.detail: He said mixed-use development can increase the town’s tax base and argued the town needs a combination of residential and commercial development.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial discipline and analytical review
stanceCards.detail: He cited his work as a fractional or consulting CFO and framed development decisions through financial discipline, design standards, and careful review.

evidence.title: Candidate profile emphasizes mixed-use development and zoning work
evidence.summary: Rossettos said that over the previous four years he worked on mixed-use developments, the unanimously approved Waterfield affordable housing project, MBTA 3A compliance zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards intended to guide development while increasing the tax base.

evidence.title: Forum message: growth and preservation can coexist
evidence.summary: At the Planning Board forum, Rossettos said growth and preservation are not mutually exclusive and centered his campaign on preserving downtown character while adding housing.

evidence.title: Cited affordable housing and 40B examples
evidence.summary: He referenced Waterfield, a completed Chapter 40B on Cambridge Street, the Washington and Swanton 40B proposal, and inclusionary units at 10 Converse Place as examples of housing work and design efforts.

evidence.title: Argued for balancing residential and commercial development
evidence.summary: Drawing on his CFO background, Rossettos said residential projects are profitable for developers and that the town needs a combination, linking development choices to fiscal impact.

evidence.title: Called some zoning rules confusing
evidence.summary: Rossettos said some zoning regulations are very complicated and confusing, suggesting interest in clearer or more workable rules.

evidence.title: 2026 election result
evidence.summary: In the March 21, 2026 town election for two Planning Board seats, Rossettos received 1,864 votes, behind Keri Layton and Amy S. Beliveau, and was not elected.

name: Shamus Brady
background: Public-school administrator and educator; former Winchester School Committee member (2021-2024); previously served on the Finance Committee during the early COVID-19 period. He also cited volunteer work with Winchester ABC, youth soccer, and the FAN Board.
summary: Brady ran for Select Board in 2026 on a platform centered on supporting an operating override, stronger fiscal stewardship, affordability relief, and efforts to attract business revenue. He emphasized his school-budget experience and prior local service. He lost the election to incumbent Michelle Prior.
locationNote: Winchester resident and father of two children in public school, according to his campaign profile.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee and Finance Committee record
priorRecordSummary: Brady pointed to an extensive public record from the School Committee, Finance Committee, and Town Meeting. In source material, he is shown supporting added school budget initiatives and asking questions about literacy resources during 2024 School Committee discussions.
keyThemes: Support for a town operating override
Public input on any service cuts

stanceCards.label: Supports operating override
stanceCards.detail: He said the town needs to pass an operating override to maintain current services and said he fully supported passage.

stanceCards.label: Calls affordability a top issue
stanceCards.detail: He said taxes and fees are too regressive and proposed an affordability committee, citing the residential tax exemption as one possible relief tool.

stanceCards.label: Seeks more business revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should more actively recruit businesses and identify revenue-generating business types that fit Winchester.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes school-budget experience
stanceCards.detail: He framed his public-schools work and School Committee service as giving him deep knowledge of the largest part of the town budget.

stanceCards.label: Public process if cuts are needed
stanceCards.detail: If an override failed, he said he would ask department heads to identify essential services and possible cuts, then bring proposals to the public through written feedback and forums.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines override, affordability, and revenue positions
evidence.summary: In his 2026 candidate profile, Brady said Winchester needs an operating override, proposed an affordability committee, supported stronger fiscal stewardship, and called for business recruitment to bring in revenue.

evidence.title: Candidate profile emphasizes school-budget and finance experience
evidence.summary: Brady said his work in public schools, School Committee service from 2021-2024, and Finance Committee experience during COVID gave him detailed knowledge of school budgets and public finance.

evidence.title: Supported additional school budget initiatives in 2024
evidence.summary: During a 2024 School Committee budget discussion, Brady expressed support for proposed initiatives serving students directly and said the committee should preserve funding room for literacy-related work.

evidence.title: Asked about literacy resources availability
evidence.summary: At a 2024 School Committee meeting, Brady asked whether the UFLI literacy resource was available to all teachers in grades K-2 across all schools.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage described campaign emphasis
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said Brady pushed for urgency, visible dissent, and new revenue streams during the Select Board campaign.

evidence.title: Lost 2026 Select Board race
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle Prior defeated Shamus Brady in the 2026 Select Board election, 2,849 to 1,159.

name: Anthea Brady
background: Select Board incumbent seeking re-election in 2025; served three years on the board, including two as vice chair. Holds a Master of Public Policy and has worked in the education sector, including special education and K-12 data.
summary: Brady ran for another Select Board term in 2025 emphasizing continuity in town governance, support for schools and staffing, capital planning, and a willingness to consider free cash or an operating override to address Winchester’s structural deficit. She was re-elected.
locationNote: Lives in the Muraco neighborhood and said she had lived in Winchester for 11 years as of Feb. 2025.
priorRecordSummary: Brady said she had served three years on the Select Board, including two as vice chair, and official May 2025 meeting minutes list her as vice-chair. She also cited prior work on ARPA subgrants, traffic and pedestrian improvements, playground funding, and bringing the CPA to Town Meeting.
keyThemes: economic development and commercial tax base

stanceCards.label: Supports school investment
stanceCards.detail: Said the school system is critical infrastructure and the town needs to support students, families, teachers, curriculum, professional development, and classroom supports.

stanceCards.label: Open to override
stanceCards.detail: Said free cash or an operating override were among the options to address the structural deficit, and described an override as potentially necessary during the 2025 campaign.

stanceCards.label: Seeks added revenue sources
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to mixed-use development, MBTA station reopening, parking return on Laraway Road, zoning changes on North Main Street, and Holton Street opportunities as ways to grow commercial activity and the tax base.

stanceCards.label: Backs affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: Called for both large- and small-scale affordable housing, including projects such as Waterfield, Washington and Swanton, Cross Street, and possible smaller-scale use at 160 Forest Street.

stanceCards.label: Supports capital and playground investments
stanceCards.detail: Cited support for safety assessment and funding for new playgrounds and said continuity around a capital plan was needed.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady said she had lived in Winchester 11 years, holds a Master of Public Policy, worked in education and K-12 data, served three years on the Select Board with two as vice chair, and supports school investment, staffing continuity, affordable housing, and economic development. She said free cash or an operating override may be needed to address the structural deficit.

evidence.title: League of Women Voters candidate debate
evidence.summary: At the March 2025 forum, Brady said the budget year was critical because costs had risen, said free cash could help in the short run, an override might be necessary, and called for continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Brady received 1,649 votes in the Select Board race, finishing among the top two candidates elected.

evidence.title: Election-night summary confirms re-election
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Paras Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady won the two Select Board seats, with Brady receiving 1,649 votes.

evidence.title: May 2025 joint meeting minutes list Brady as vice-chair
evidence.summary: Official State of the Town meeting minutes list Anthea Brady as vice-chair of the Select Board in May 2025.

evidence.title: Town Meeting remarks on school budget
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting debate on the school budget, Brady said the schools obviously need help with math, that the McCall schedule needs to come into compliance with the rest of the district, and that the town should invest now.

name: Christian Nixon
background: Architect with two degrees from Rice University and an education policy fellowship from Northeastern University’s Institute for Educational Leadership. He said he oversees budgeting, design, and construction of capital projects for a nearby community and school district, with more than 25 years of experience in K-12 education and healthcare project delivery.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member and longtime Town Meeting member who emphasized school funding, facilities planning, sustainable town revenue, labor relations, and district governance experience.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 26 years, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Nixon said he has served on Town Meeting since 2008 and on the School Committee since 2010, along with service on the EFPBC, Capital Planning Committee, Audit Advisory Committee, and school facilities working groups.

stanceCards.label: Advocates for full FY26 funding case
stanceCards.detail: Listed making the case for WPS’s true FY26 funding needs as a top priority and cited use of the special education reserve fund to protect the FY25 operating budget.

stanceCards.label: Supports sustainable revenue planning
stanceCards.detail: Said he supports the State of the Town’s goals toward sustainable revenue models to meet long-term town and district needs.

stanceCards.label: Focus on facilities projects
stanceCards.detail: Prioritized the New Muraco MSBA statement of interest, Phase II of the 10-Year Facilities Master Plan, and funding for Lincoln Elementary improvements including HVAC and sitework.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes governance and budgeting systems
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to his long service on town boards and described understanding board roles, collaboration, and the SPED reserve fund’s check-and-balance structure as important to effective government.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Nixon described his professional background, long town service, and priorities including FY26 funding, sustainable revenue, facilities planning, labor negotiations, and Lincoln Elementary improvements.

evidence.title: Budget answer cites use of SPED reserve fund
evidence.summary: He said the School Committee approved drawing more than $360,000 from the Special Education Reserve Fund to cover unbudgeted FY25 special education costs and protect educational quality.

evidence.title: Incumbent School Committee and town service
evidence.summary: In his profile, Nixon said he has served on Town Meeting since 2008 and on the School Committee since 2010, plus multiple finance and facilities bodies.

evidence.title: Official election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Nixon received 975 votes in the 2025 School Committee race, behind Stefanie Mnayarji and Karen Bolognese, the two candidates elected.

evidence.title: Election-night recap confirms outcome
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, while incumbent Christian Nixon received 975 votes.

name: Dorothy R. Simboli
background: Lawyer with commercial and residential real estate experience; has lived in Winchester 25 years. Served as chair of the Winchester Zoning Board of Appeals since March 2024, after prior service as a full member and associate member, and has been a Town Meeting member since 2016.
summary: Select Board candidate who emphasized fiscal planning, transparent budgeting, business-friendly development, affordable housing, and updated school curriculum and performance measures. She lost the 2025 Select Board race.
locationNote: Winchester Town Meeting member from Precinct 6; longtime local service on the Zoning Board of Appeals.
priorRecordSummary: Simboli’s prior local record is in land use and town governance rather than on the Select Board itself: Zoning Board of Appeals service since 2016, including chair since 2024, plus Town Meeting service since 2016.
keyThemes: Updated curriculum and school performance measures

stanceCards.label: Scrutinize school spending
stanceCards.detail: Said the school budget should receive a detailed review, especially major cost drivers such as special education, while maintaining essential services.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy improvement with accountability
stanceCards.detail: Called for updated curriculum and later, as a Town Meeting member, asked how the literacy pilot would determine success.

stanceCards.label: Planning for major school capital needs
stanceCards.detail: Identified Lynch and future Muraco school projects as part of the town’s deferred capital backlog requiring sound fiscal planning.

stanceCards.label: Expand commercial tax base
stanceCards.detail: Advocated making Winchester more business-friendly and streamlining permitting to encourage commercial development, especially along North Main Street.

stanceCards.label: Open to a right-sized override
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said the town should consider an override but that it needed to be strategically planned and right sized.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: background and local service
evidence.summary: Simboli described herself as a lawyer with real estate experience, said she had lived in Winchester for 25 years, and listed service on the Zoning Board of Appeals and Town Meeting.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: priorities on schools and finances
evidence.summary: She cited updated curriculum, literacy, math, professional development, special education, and support for teachers and staff, along with transparent budgeting, prudent resource allocation, and financial stability amid deferred capital projects.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: structural deficit response
evidence.summary: She said the structural deficit required detailed budget evaluation, maintaining the bond rating, considering measures such as rebidding health insurance, and taking a deep dive into school budget line items including special education costs.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: affordable housing and business development
evidence.summary: She supported collaboratively planned affordable housing, including at 160 Forest Street, and proposed streamlining permitting and addressing parking to grow the commercial tax base.

evidence.title: Select Board forum: override and long-term planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters debate, Simboli said the town should consider an override, but that it must be right sized and part of strategic planning; she also said she wanted earlier involvement in the budget process.

name: Jack LeMenager
background: Winchester resident since 1988. He has served on the Historical Commission since 2009 and has been chair for the past eight years. He has also been a Town Meeting member for 15 years and serves on the executive board of the Town Meeting Members Association. He is a recent retiree and previously worked for management consulting firms on organizational change and employee communications.
summary: LeMenager ran a write-in campaign for Planning Board, arguing that Winchester should encourage more mixed-use, commercial, and residential development while preserving architectural integrity. He said he would prioritize better coordination among town boards and closer work with the Select Board to support rational, right-size growth. He won the open Planning Board seat in 2025.
locationNote: Longtime Winchester resident; moved to town in 1988 and raised two sons who attended Winchester schools.
priorRecordLabel: Historical Commission chair; Town Meeting member
priorRecordSummary: His prior public record is in related local governance roles rather than on the Planning Board itself: Historical Commission member since 2009, chair for eight years, Town Meeting member for 15 years, and participant on temporary and ad hoc town committees including the train station study committee and the Community Preservation Act Advisory Committee.

stanceCards.label: More coordination across boards
stanceCards.detail: He said boards and commissions should work together more closely and that, if elected, he would prioritize more communication and cross-fertilization of ideas, especially between the Planning Board and Select Board.

stanceCards.label: Support for commercial development
stanceCards.detail: He said it is critical to encourage more commercial and residential development and specifically called for more commercial space in areas such as North Main.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources do not describe a position on school facilities or school operations.

stanceCards.label: Favors more housing development, with limits noted
stanceCards.detail: He supported greater residential and mixed-use development, while noting Winchester has limited developable space and emphasizing development that fits the town's built environment.

evidence.title: Campaign profile outlines development and coordination priorities
evidence.summary: In his candidate profile, LeMenager said Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development, simplify the approval track where possible, and improve coordination among boards while preserving architectural integrity.

evidence.title: Longtime service in town government and preservation roles
evidence.summary: The profile says he has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, including eight years as chair, has been a Town Meeting member for 15 years, and has served on several town committees.

evidence.title: Official results show LeMenager led Planning Board write-in race
evidence.summary: Official election results list Jack LeMenager with 739 votes in the Planning Board race, ahead of Matthew Zarracina with 146 votes.

evidence.title: Election-night report says he won open Planning Board seat
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that LeMenager won the open Planning Board seat being vacated by Diab Jerius with 739 write-in votes.

name: Karen Maruyama Bolognese
background: Incumbent School Committee member first elected in 2019 and chair in 2024-25; also a Town Meeting member since 2012. Professional background includes veterinary medicine, finance, translation/interpretation, and teaching. Education includes a DVM from Tufts, an MBA from the University of Michigan, and a BA in English from Vanderbilt.
summary: Karen Maruyama Bolognese ran for reelection to the School Committee in 2025 as an incumbent emphasizing long-term budgeting, support for district priorities, and a process-driven approach to curriculum changes. She highlighted both educational opportunity for students and fiscal sustainability for taxpayers.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 14 years.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent School Committee chair and member
priorRecordSummary: Served on the School Committee since 2019 and as chair in 2021-22 and 2024-25. Also served on several town working groups and as a Town Meeting member.
keyThemes: Fiscal responsibility and tax burden awareness
Support for literacy review led by educators
Student opportunity across academics, arts, sports, and special education

stanceCards.label: Long-term, planful budgeting
stanceCards.detail: Advocated a long-term budgeting approach and participation in the five-year strategic plan and budget process, Winchester 2030, to align staffing, programming, professional development, and facilities needs.

stanceCards.label: Acknowledges resident tax burden
stanceCards.detail: Said she takes fiscal responsibility seriously and wants school budgets to support priorities while remaining financially sustainable for the town over the long term.

stanceCards.label: Backs educator-led literacy review
stanceCards.detail: Said she fully supports the superintendent's literacy approach, with elementary educators and specialists reviewing materials and piloting options before School Committee action.

stanceCards.label: Favors controls and education over a total ban
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said phones are a distraction and should have controls at home, but argued students also need real-world skills and emphasized education about wise device use.

stanceCards.label: Links facilities to long-term district planning
stanceCards.detail: Included school facilities among the critical long-term district needs she has worked to prioritize.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: background and priorities
evidence.summary: Outlined her professional and civic background, including School Committee service since 2019, and said her candidacy is motivated by meeting diverse student needs while maintaining fiscal responsibility and long-term sustainability.

evidence.title: Budget approach in candidate questionnaire
evidence.summary: Said she has advocated a long-term, planful approach to budgeting through Winchester 2030 and has worked with district leaders and stakeholders to prioritize staffing, programming, professional development, and facilities.

evidence.title: Literacy curriculum response
evidence.summary: Said she fully supports the superintendent's literacy process, with educators and specialists reviewing materials, assessments, and professional development and piloting options before recommendations come to the committee.

evidence.title: Candidate forum on phone-free schools
evidence.summary: Said district practice is that phones stay in backpacks and smartwatches in school mode, but argued a total device ban would be difficult to enforce; she emphasized partnership with families and education about wise usage.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Karen Bolognese won one of two School Committee seats with 1,512 votes, behind Stefanie Mnayarji and ahead of incumbent Christian Nixon.

evidence.title: Post-election committee minutes
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 School Committee and Select Board meeting minutes list Karen Maruyama Bolognese as School Committee chair, consistent with her continuing in office after the election.

name: Paras Bhayani
background: Corporate finance and business strategy professional with prior public-sector budget experience, including leading a $1.7 billion operating and capital budget for the Chicago Transit Authority. He has lived in Winchester for five years and said he was drawn to the town by its schools and community. He also described himself as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change, a parent-led group focused on early literacy curriculum.
summary: Bhayani ran on fiscal stewardship, pairing efficiency measures with likely new revenue, and emphasized long-term planning for both operating budgets and capital needs. He also backed transit-oriented infill housing, small-business support, and technology upgrades to streamline town processes.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester with his wife and two young daughters; said he has lived in town for five years.
priorRecordLabel: Finance and civic involvement, but no prior Winchester elected office cited
priorRecordSummary: Bhayani did not cite prior elected or appointed Winchester town office in the profile, but pointed to finance leadership in Chicago public-sector budgeting and local involvement as a founding member of an early-literacy parent group.

stanceCards.label: Balance school and town funding with added revenue if needed
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester needs a responsible, balanced approach to town and school funding and that efficiencies alone would likely not be enough to meet operating and capital needs.

stanceCards.label: Open to additional override revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said the 2019 override has lasted longer than planned and that additional revenue will likely be needed; at the candidate forum he said taxpayers would likely need to be asked for more money after efficiencies are found.

stanceCards.label: Use technology to improve efficiency
stanceCards.detail: He proposed off-the-shelf technology to automate town processes and digitize permits, approvals, applications, and other business-facing systems.

stanceCards.label: Calls for a capital plan and possible dedicated funding
stanceCards.detail: He pointed to a large backlog of capital projects and, at the forum, referred to a five-year plan and an override dedicated for capital projects.

stanceCards.label: Supports infill and affordable housing on developed land
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester faces an affordability crisis and backed thoughtful infill development, completion of already funded projects, and redevelopment of already developed land rather than wooded open space.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Bhayani described his finance background, early literacy involvement, and campaign priorities including balanced funding, capital projects, housing, economic development, and sustainability. He also supported efficiencies plus likely new revenue and proposed technology-driven process improvements.

evidence.title: Select Board candidate debate focused on budget shortfall
evidence.summary: At the March 5 forum, Bhayani said Winchester faced a challenging fiscal reality, called for a comprehensive long-term plan, said efficiencies should be identified first, and then taxpayers would likely need to provide additional money. He also referenced a five-year capital plan and an override dedicated for capital projects.

evidence.title: Housing and economic development positions in profile
evidence.summary: He backed thoughtful infill housing, supported projects near transit and town center, said 160 Forest Street could model small-scale affordable housing, and proposed mixed-use development, parking solutions, and streamlined permitting to support small businesses.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Bhayani received 1,945 votes in the Select Board race, the highest total in the four-candidate field for two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting identifies winners
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady won the two Select Board seats, with Bhayani receiving 1,945 votes.

evidence.title: May 30 State of the Town minutes list him as a Select Board member
evidence.summary: Town meeting minutes from May 30, 2025 list Paras Bhayani as present with the Select Board, consistent with his having taken office after the election.

name: Rick Welch
background: Real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including permitting and development work on multiple 40B projects and senior living housing as president of Mesiti Development Corporation. Winchester resident for 35 years.
summary: Welch presented himself as a development-oriented Select Board candidate focused on helping with town real estate matters, affordable housing, housing for seniors, and expanding the commercial tax base. He said the town should address the structural deficit without cutting school or sports programs.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester; said he has lived in town for 35 years and that his three children graduated from Winchester Public Schools.
priorRecordSummary: The available sources show campaign statements and final election results, but no prior elected record or vote history in town office.
keyThemes: Protecting school and sports programs while addressing the deficit
Commercial tax base growth through mixed-use and 55-plus development

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and senior housing
stanceCards.detail: Welch said his affordable-housing development experience could help Winchester navigate housing needs and said he wants 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities for seniors struggling to remain in town.

stanceCards.label: Wants to avoid cuts to schools
stanceCards.detail: He said the deficit is concerning but that the town should look for ways to close it without touching school or sports programs.

stanceCards.label: Favors tax-base growth through development
stanceCards.detail: He proposed expanding the downtown center with lower-level commercial space and upper-floor mixed residential uses, and said 55-plus projects can bring in tax dollars.

stanceCards.label: Concerned about the structural deficit
stanceCards.detail: Welch described the increased budget deficit as concerning and said the town needs to look closely at ways to make up for it while maintaining current standards.

evidence.title: Professional background in development and housing
evidence.summary: Welch said he is a real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including multiple 40B projects and senior living development.

evidence.title: Reason for running and focus on housing and town real estate
evidence.summary: He said he wants to use his development experience to help the town with real estate deals, affordable housing needs, and housing options for local seniors.

evidence.title: Deficit response without school or sports cuts
evidence.summary: Welch said the budget deficit is concerning and that the town should find ways to address it without touching school or sports programs.

evidence.title: 160 Forest Street and senior housing
evidence.summary: He said he wants 160 Forest Street to provide opportunity for Winchester seniors struggling to stay in their hometown.

evidence.title: Commercial tax base and mixed-use development ideas
evidence.summary: Welch said downtown expansion could include lower-level commercial space with upper-floor retail and residential uses, reduced parking expectations, and incentives for 55-plus projects to increase tax revenue.

evidence.title: Lost 2025 Select Board race
evidence.summary: Official results show Richard Welch, Jr. received 674 votes in the Select Board race, behind Paras Bhayani (1,945), Anthea Brady (1,649), and Dorothy Simboli (806), with two seats to be elected.

name: Stefanie Mnayarji
background: Economist with education from the London School of Economics and Northeastern University; worked in FinTech consulting and later founded a Boston business. Lived in Winchester for eight years at the time of her 2025 candidate profile.
summary: Stefanie Mnayarji won a seat in the 2025 School Committee race. In her campaign materials and forum comments, she emphasized literacy reform, broader curriculum review, intentional technology use including stricter device policies, and budget decisions that protect classroom investments through a data-driven and collaborative process.
locationNote: Winchester resident; said she had lived in town for eight years and had been elected as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member the prior spring.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting Member and school-community volunteer roles
priorRecordSummary: Before joining the School Committee, Mnayarji said she served as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member and held roles including co-chair of Parents' Inter-School Council, TMMA Board, Ambrose School Council, and Ambrose PTO president. A May 2025 town meeting record shows her serving on the School Committee as Recording Secretary after the election.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: She identified literacy reform as a core reason for running and said curriculum updates should include classroom investments, coaching, and professional development.

stanceCards.label: Favors stricter device limits
stanceCards.detail: She called for intentional technology use, review of personal device policies, and at the candidate forum said she supported a phone-free, more broadly device-free, school experience.

stanceCards.label: Protect classroom investments
stanceCards.detail: She said budget management should be data-driven and collaborative, with priority given to classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curricula, teacher support, and special education.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes transparency and collaboration
stanceCards.detail: She said budget decisions should involve town boards, teachers, parents, and community members, with clear communication about challenges and funding solutions.

stanceCards.label: Links device policy to student well-being
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, she said phone-free districts report better focus and improved mental health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Mnayarji described her professional background, local civic roles, and campaign focus on funding, curriculum, literacy reform, technology use, and school culture.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on budget management
evidence.summary: She called for data-driven resource allocation, grant-seeking, and transparent, collaborative decision-making, while prioritizing classroom investments and administrative efficiencies over cuts to instruction.

evidence.title: Forum comments on device-free schools
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Mnayarji said she supported a phone-free and more broadly device-free school experience, arguing it could improve focus and mental health.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: In the School Committee race for two seats, Mnayarji received 1,964 votes, the top total among the three candidates listed.

evidence.title: Post-election School Committee membership
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 State of the Town minutes list Stefanie Mnayarji as School Committee Recording Secretary, confirming she was serving on the committee after the election.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting confirms outcome
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Mnayarji and Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats, while Christian Nixon lost.
